    print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=[{min(rows)},{min(cols)}]-[{max(rows)},{max(cols)}]")

print("\nAFTER nodes:")
for n in after['nodes']:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=[{min(rows)},{min(cols)}]-[{max(rows)},{max(cols)}]")
</parameter>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The main change is:
- Before: Node 0 was Pink (P) with 64 pixels at row 0, full width
- After: Node 0 is Pink (P) with 63 pixels (one less), and a new Node 1 is charcoal (c) with 1 pixel at [0,63]
So clicking on the charcoal shape caused one pixel of the pink bar at the top to change to charcoal. The pink bar shrunk from 64 to 63 pixels, and the rightmost pixel (col 63) turned charcoal.
This suggests the pink bar is some kind of "lives" or "progress" bar, and clicking on charcoal objects converts pink pixels to charcoal. But wait - the charcoal shape itself (node 10, 16 pixels) didn't change. So maybe clicking on charcoal objects "marks" them somehow?
Let me think about this differently. The pink bar at the top seems to be a counter. Clicking on the charcoal object caused one pink pixel to turn charcoal. Maybe the goal is to turn all pink pixels to charcoal by clicking on charcoal objects?
But the charcoal object itself didn't disappear. Let me try clicking on it again to see if more pink pixels change.

Let me try clicking on the charcoal shape again.
